narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.30—slightly above the typical 3.21 average—your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on past students' data to predict bar exam success on the first try. The model assessed your profile, including your LSAT score of 34, middle family income bracket, part-time enrollment status, and school decile rankings of 5th in Year 1 and 4th in Year 3, both below the dataset averages. It calculated a 52.9% probability that you would fail the bar exam, tipping the scales just over the threshold that led to your application's rejection, as the school prioritizes admitting those predicted to pass confidently. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your choice of part-time status, which is uncommon—only about 7% of students opt for it—and the model sees it as a notable risk compared to the 93% who go full-time and tend to perform better. Your LSAT score, while respectable, worked in your favor by pulling the prediction toward passing, as it suggested stronger potential than many applicants. Similarly, your above-average undergrad GPA provided a positive boost, helping offset some concerns and showing your academic capability. Overall, these top three elements—part-time enrollment as the strongest drag, tempered by your LSAT and GPA—dominated the model's decision, outweighing other details like your family background. In comparison to typical students who pass, your part-time path stands out as riskier in the model's eyes, even with your decent grades. This isn't a judgment on your abilities or background, but a data-driven call based on patterns from thousands of past bar takers. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it closer to the 36.7 average, exploring full-time options if feasible, or strengthening your Year 3 decile through targeted academic efforts. You might also gather letters highlighting your resilience as a part-time student or seek programs with flexible tracks proven to support bar passage. With adjustments like these, reapplying could shift your predicted odds significantly in your favor.
